input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A laboratory‑scale gantry crane comprising a steel frame, linear rails, rotary joints, a hoist and an embedded controller. Operated in a university lab for research and education, it features a well‑defined mechanical kinematic chain, modular payload mounting, and accessible sensors (encoders and load cells) that enable precise state estimation and closed‑loop control. Its compact size, repeatable motion, and readily available CAD geometry make it an exemplary candidate for digital twinning, allowing accurate modeling and real‑time data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides a suite of services: real‑time monitoring of joint positions, payload weight and actuator currents; an optimal‑control service that computes feedforward commands to minimize motion time while respecting torque limits; historical data logging that archives time‑stamped sensor measurements for trend and fault analysis; interactive data visualization dashboards displaying trajectory plots and error indicators; and continuous‑validation routines that compare measured and simulated states to detect modeling discrepancies. These services are available to researchers, educators, and the physical system via a bi‑directional data interface.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies exclusively on freely available, commonly used software. CAD geometry is generated in open‑source CAD platforms, kinematic modeling and control logic are implemented in standard programming environments using widely adopted numerical libraries, real‑time data acquisition and logging are achieved through open communication protocols (e.g., serial, MQTT), and visualization is rendered in web‑based dashboards. Continuous validation and simulation are performed within open‑source simulation environments, ensuring full reproducibility and ease of integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: GreenhouseDT employs a three-tiered architecture comprising the physical greenhouse equipped with sensors, a virtual machine-based digital twin environment, and a backend services layer. Communication between the greenhouse and the twin is mediated by an ActiveMQ message broker that handles asynchronous messaging for sensor telemetry and twin commands. Telemetry data are stored in an InfluxDB time‑series database, providing persistent, time‑ordered storage for monitoring and analysis. The digital twin logic, including its adaptation algorithms, is encapsulated within software components deployed in virtual machines, ensuring reproducibility and ease of installation via pre‑configured VM images.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The exemplar utilizes Azure-based tools for its digital twin pipeline, indicating integration with Microsoft Azure services. Internally, data flow is orchestrated through an ActiveMQ broker and an InfluxDB database, while external interfaces are exposed via standard protocols such as HTTPS for RESTful access and MQTT for device communication, as suggested by the use of Raspberry‑Pi sensor nodes and Azure tools. The system’s code is hosted on GitHub, enabling source‑level integration and version control interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The GreenhouseDT exemplar implements a behavioral digital twin comprising runtime adaptation models for both the physical greenhouse and its virtual counterpart. These models ingest sensor and actuator data streamed via an ActiveMQ message broker into an InfluxDB time‑series database, which stores pre‑recorded data packaged in the virtual machine images. The digital twin composition includes a simulation component that consumes the InfluxDB outputs to generate predictive state trajectories. In the Air Quality Management exemplar, the digital twin is defined in AutomationML and orchestrated through Microsoft Azure services. Sensor data collected by a Raspberry‑Pi‑based sensing platform are streamed to Azure IoT Hub, where Azure Stream Analytics processes the data and Azure Digital Twins maintains a real‑time geometric and behavioral representation for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Logging Application, implemented in Python, continuously reads the crane’s state via the Physical Crane Interface and writes state records to a TimescaleDB database using standard PostgreSQL operations. No additional preprocessing or data‑quality measures are described; the raw state data as provided by the Physical Crane Interface is stored directly. The stored data is subsequently consumed by the Grafana Dashboard for visualization. Specific data types, transmission frequencies, or event triggers are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Control Application are dispatched through the Python‑based Physical Crane Interface to the crane controllers. Commands are sent as direct function calls over the interface; specific message formats, authentication, or validation steps are not described in the document. The system relies on the underlying Physical Crane Interface to handle low‑level communication protocols, while the Logging Application provides feedback by recording the resulting state for monitoring. No explicit conflict resolution logic or command execution confirmation protocol is detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Digital twin services communicate over an MQTT broker implementing the MQTT protocol, serving as the pub/sub middleware between components such as Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Validation, and Dashboard. The Physical Crane Interface connects to the crane controllers via a Python library; the specific underlying protocol (e.g., serial, Ethernet/IP) is not specified. Network topology is implicitly a local network with all services running as containers on the same host; no explicit bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security measures are documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system comprises multiple Python‑based containers (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human Machine Interface, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Validation, Dashboard) but the deployment environment—whether on‑premises edge hardware, cloud, or hybrid—is not specified in the document. Computational, storage, scalability, and availability requirements are likewise not described.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar offers a suite of decision‑support capabilities that combine real‑time data acquisition, simulation, and prediction. Data from a Raspberry‑Pi‑based sensor array is streamed via MQTT over ActiveMQ into an Azure IoT Hub, where it feeds into Azure Stream Analytics for live processing. A web‑based dashboard delivers runtime visualizations of pollutant concentrations and temperature/humidity metrics. Predictive analytics built on Azure Machine Learning extrapolate future indoor air quality under varying occupancy and ventilation scenarios, thereby providing quantified risk assessments for virus‑spread mitigation. Users interact with the system through the dashboard, select simulation scenarios, and receive confidence scores derived from the underlying probabilistic models. Decision logic is triggered when threshold values are exceeded, automatically logging events and prompting recommended interventions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The exemplar implements an AutomationML‑based digital twin model, leveraging the AutomationML specification to encode plant and component hierarchy, process data, and enable interoperability with other PLM and manufacturing systems. It also adheres to common IoT communication standards: sensor data is published over MQTT via ActiveMQ, and data ingestion into Azure utilizes the Azure IoT Hub interface, which supports industry‑standard protocols such as AMQP and HTTPS. No explicit certifications or regulatory compliance frameworks are reported.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
